coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots-embedded graphitic carbon nitride nanotubes for enhancing the power conversion efficiency of sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Riaz, S.
Park, S.-J.
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (CN) has been widely used as a photocatalyst. Very few researchers have reported the use of CN in quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSCs). In this study, we prepared nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dot (CQD)-embedded CN nanotubes (CCNTs) with freeze-dried urea and CQD precursors. The prepared CCNTs were used as efficient light harvesters in QDSCs for the first time; their use significantly improved the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the solar cells compared to those of CQD, CN NT, and bulk CN-sensitized solar cells. The CCNT-sensitized solar cell exhibits 1.01% PCE, which is the highest value among carbon-based QDSCs. Moreover, the CCNTs-sensitized device showed superior photostability over those of CQDs-, CN NTs-, and bulk CN-sensitized devices. The improved performance of the CCNT-sensitized solar cell can be attributed to the facilitated photoelectron transport and suppressed charge recombination. The integration of nitrogen-rich CQDs in CCNTs adjusts the band alignment and maximizes the visible light harvest by reducing the energy barriers, which improves the charge collection efficiency of the device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photostability
current density
bannd adjustment
implantation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, bulk CN, CNNTs, and CCNTs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Technology Innovation Program (or Industrial Strategic Technology Development Program) ( 20012373 , Innovative technology for CO2 free hydrogen production using molten catalysts) funded By the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE, Korea). The research was also supported the Korea Industrial Complex Corporation through the Project to Program of Industrial Complex Clusters ( IRIC2101 , Industry-University-Research joint R&D support) funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE, Korea).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram for synthesis of CCNTs, TEM images of (b) CQDs, (c) CN NTs, (d) CCNTs, (e and f) high resolution TEM images of selected parts from CCNTs.
(a) UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, bulk CN, and CCNTs, (b) Full valence band spectrum of CCNTs, (c) energy band diagram of CCNTs, and (d) PL spectra of bulk CN and CCNTs.
(a) XPS survey spectra and high resolution (b) N1s, (c) C1s spectra of bulk CN and CCNTs, and (d) different types of N1s in CCNTs.
FTIR spectra of bulk CN, CQDs, and CCNTs.
(a) J–V curves, (b) Transient photocurrent density measurements, under 100 mW/cm2 light illumination.
(a) Device structure (b) Energy band positions of all the materials.
Nyquist plots of all the devices measured at different applied voltages (a) Device-I, (b) Device-II, (c) Device-III, and (d) Device-IV.
(a) Chemical capacitance (Cμ) and (b) Recombination resistance (Rrec) on applied voltages (V).
Photovoltaic parameters measured under 100 mW/cm−2 light intensity.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots-embedded graphitic carbon nitride nanotubes for enhancing the power conversion efficiency of sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100 Inharo, Incheon 22212, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100 Inharo, Incheon 22212, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (CN) has been widely used as a photocatalyst. Very few researchers have reported the use of CN in quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSCs). In this study, we prepared nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dot (CQD)-embedded CN nanotubes (CCNTs) with freeze-dried urea and CQD precursors. The prepared CCNTs were used as efficient light harvesters in QDSCs for the first time; their use significantly improved the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the solar cells compared to those of CQD, CN NT, and bulk CN-sensitized solar cells. The CCNT-sensitized solar cell exhibits 1.01% PCE, which is the highest value among carbon-based QDSCs. Moreover, the CCNTs-sensitized device showed superior photostability over those of CQDs-, CN NTs-, and bulk CN-sensitized devices. The improved performance of the CCNT-sensitized solar cell can be attributed to the facilitated photoelectron transport and suppressed charge recombination. The integration of nitrogen-rich CQDs in CCNTs adjusts the band alignment and maximizes the visible light harvest by reducing the energy barriers, which improves the charge collection efficiency of the device.

ce:section.ce:para:
The exceptional properties of quantum dots (QDs) such as the bandgap tunability, high intrinsic dipole moment, solution processability, and significant extinction coefficients make them superior to metalorganic dyes; therefore, they are a promising alternative for light absorption [1–6]. Although the power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSCs) are inferior to those of dye-sensitized solar cells, QDSCs have attracted increasing interest among researchers as promising low-cost third-generation solar cell candidates; therefore, this field is progressing very rapidly [7–10]. At present, research on QDSCs primarily focuses on toxic chalcogenides (cadmium and lead); their commercial application is impossible owing to the related environmental and health issues [11]. Moreover, “green” alternatives on Pb and Cd-free devices have been proposed [12]. In addition, the synthesis of these inorganic semiconductor QDs with controlled sizes and shapes involves complicated processes [13,14]. Because these QDs cause corrosion in I−/
I 3 −
electrolyte and affect the stability, which decreases the PCE of PV devices, researchers should develop alternatives to conventional Pb or Cd chalcogenide-based QDs; these alternatives should be environmentally friendly and exhibit good optoelectronic properties for the fabrication of eco-friendly solar devices [15,16].

Carbon-based nanoparticles (including graphene quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs)) have been proposed as cheap, biocompatible, and non-toxic alternatives to the abovementioned toxic materials [17–19]. Owing to their low toxicity, excellent optical properties, low cost, easy synthesis, and inertness, these carbon-based materials are widely used in light-emitting diodes, photocatalysis, and PV devices [20–24]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are also widely used as sensitizers or co-sensitizers in inorganic QD-based solar cells. Inorganic QD-based solar cell devices in which carbon-based nanoparticles were used as co-sensitizers exhibited an improvement in their PCE from 0.49% to 12.75% [25–28,66]. However, sensitized CQD-based solar cells have achieved a maximum PCE of 0.87% at 100 mW/cm−2 light intensity [29,30] (a comparison is provided in Table S1). The lower PCE of CQD-sensitized solar cells (CSSCs) may be due to the low short-circuit current density (Jsc); this can be due to the larger gap between the conduction bands (CBs) of TiO2 and CQDs, which results in the recombination of the majority of photoinduced carriers rather than their transfer to the CB of TiO2. Moreover, CQDs exhibit a narrower absorption band in the visible light range than in the ultraviolet range; this can also be a reason for the decreased PCE [31,32]. Heteroatom (nitrogen and sulfur) doping is one of the popular ways for reducing the gap between the CBs of TiO2 and CQDs by creating sub-electronic levels inside the band gap. Much effort has been devoted to fabricating N-doped CQDs as “green” sensitizers for CQDs-sensitized solar cells [33]. Nevertheless, the PCE of CQD-sensitized solar cells must be further improved.

Nanostructured carbon nitrides (CNs) have been focused due to their application as metal-free semiconductors with an approximately 2.7 eV band gap [34]. CN is widely used as a photocatalyst and in electrocatalysis and heterogeneous catalysis for oxygen reduction and organic synthesis [35,36]. CN exhibits low cost and a high specific surface area; in addition, it allows structural modifications via surface engineering. However, few researchers have applied CN in PV [37]. In this field, CN has proved itself as an excellent low-cost renewable energy source with high flexibility [38–40]. Researchers have applied bulk CN or CNQDs in mesostructured solar cells as acceptors and reported that the PCEs of the solar cell devices were improved. Byers et al. [41] applied CN thin films deposited on a conjugated polymer and discovered that CN functions as an acceptor that generates a photocurrent. Chen et al. [42] incorporated CNQDs into bulk heterojunction solar cells and reported that the PCEs of the solar cell devices were better than those of their undoped counterparts. Ansari et al. [40] reported the photo-booster effect of nanostructured CN on the PV performance of CdS QD-sensitized solar cells and observed 32% improvement in the efficiency of CNNT-based solar cell devices then CN nanoflake-based devices. Motivated by the previously presented results, we prepared CQD-embedded CN nanotubes (CCNTs) and used them as a light-absorbing layer in CQD-sensitized solar cells without the need of additional sensitizing material. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study in which CCNTs are used as the only absorbing/photoactive layer in CQD-sensitized solar cells with 1.01% PCE. The improved PV performance of the CQD-sensitized solar cells is attributed to the enhanced interfacial interaction in the active layer and hole transport layer, facilitated photoinduced electron transfer, and improved charge transport.
All the materials were of analytical grade and used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs, bulk CN, CNNTs, and CCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen-rich CQDs were prepared with the method reported in the study by Wang et al. [43]. Briefly, specific amounts of citric acid and ethylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL distilled water and stirred for 30 min. The solution was poured into an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. The obtained brown product was dialyzed to remove larger particles and impurities.

To prepare bulk CN, 5 g urea was inserted into an aluminum crucible and heated to 450 °C at 0.5 °C/min in a muffle furnace; this temperature was maintained for 5 h. Yellow-colored bulk CN was obtained after letting the sample cool down to room temperature. To prepare CNNTs, bulk CN was exfoliated via ultrasonication in water for 2 h and then centrifuged and dried. The dried sample was heated at 400 °C for 30 min to increase the distance between the layers. Finally, the obtained powder sample was quenched in 500 ml cold water. The thermal pressure on CN surface led to the shrinkage of the flakes and formation of CNNTs. The CNNTs were obtained via centrifugation followed by freeze-drying.

The CCNTs were prepared with the method reported in the study by Wang et al. [43]. Briefly, urea (5 g) and 15 mg of CQDs were added in 50 mL water; the mixture was treated in liquid nitrogen and subsequently freeze-dried. The obtained nanoscale precursors were calcined at 550 °C for 3 h. The obtained samples were quenched in cold water and subsequently freeze-dried.

The cleaned FTO with an active area of 0.25 cm2 was treated in 40 mM TiCl4 aqueous solution at 80 °C for 1 h; subsequently, it was washed with water and anhydrous ethanol. In the next step, a 20 nm-sized TiO2 paste with a thickness of around 10 μm was deposited via doctor blading and dried at 100 °C. A 200 nm-sized TiO2 paste was placed on top of the first layer; the film was sintered at 500 °C for 30 min. After letting the sample cool to 80 °C, the TiO2 film was treated in aqueous CCNTs solution at room temperature for 24 h; subsequently, it was washed with ethanol and dried. The solar cell was prepared by sandwiching I−/
I 3 −
(Iodolyte Z-50, Solaronix) electrolyte between a CCNTs-sensitized working electrode and a Pt counter electrode (Device-I). Device-II, Device-III, and Device-IV were prepared by following a similar protocol, except the working electrodes were replaced with CQDs, CNNTs, and bulk CN-sensitized electrodes. Five solar cell devices were prepared for each experiment, and their average PCEs were recorded.

In this study, tubular CCNTs were prepared. According to the study by Wang et al.[43], embedded CQDs can facilitate the assembly of tubular structures in the co-condensation process, thereby resulting in the formation of micro-regional heterostructures that promote photocarrier separation and photoelectron transport. The CCNTs were prepared as schematically shown in Fig. 1 a. The amino groups in CQDs facilitate the interfacial interaction between CN and CQDs via amide bonds, which results in the formation of CCNTs. Furthermore, the implantation of CQDs in CN facilitates the formation of tubular CCNT structures. The morphologies and structures of all the prepared nanostructures were investigated via field emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM). Fig. 1 shows the TEM images of the prepared CQDs, CNNTs, and CCNTs. As shown in Fig. 1b, the amine-rich CQDs have a uniform size distribution with varying diameters ranging from 1.5 to 4 nm (the average diameter is 2.1 nm). TEM image of the CNNTs are shown in Fig. 1c. The pristine CNNTs have 20–70 nm diameters and 7–8 nm thick walls. Fig. 1d shows the tubular morphology of the CCNTs; they have even surfaces without a significant nanoparticle development. Moreover, the CCNTs have less than 20 nm thick walls. The HR-TEM images are shown in Fig. 1e and f. Many small QDs can be seen on the hollow CN tubular structures; they have resulted in the formation of micro-regional heterostructures between graphitic carbons of CQDs and CN [44]. Fig. 1f shows the lattice fringe image of the CQDs with spacing of 0.18 nm, which correspond to the (100) diffraction facet of graphitic carbon [45].

Fig. 2 shows the UV–Vis spectra of CQDs, bulk CN, and CCNTs. The energy band gaps of all the materials were measured from Tauc plots obtained with the Kubelka–Munk function. As seen in the spectra, the CCNTs exhibit a red-shift compared to the spectra of the CQDs and bulk CN. Bulk CN, CNNTs and CQDs exhibited the absorption edges at 449, 452, and 457 nm, which correspond to approximately 2.76, 2.73, and 2.71 eV band gaps (Inset, Fig. 2a, and Fig. S1), respectively. The red shift in the absorption edges of the CCNTs results in a reduced band gap of approximately 2.65. The reduction in the band gap can be related to the quantum confinement effect in the CCNTs. Moreover, the absorption in the 450–800 nm range promotes the generation of photocarriers. The XPS valence band (VB) spectra of the CCNTs, bulk CN, and CQDs, are shown in Fig. 2b, S2a, and S3a, respectively. The 2 b, VB of the CCNTs is shifted by 0.19 and 0.13 eV (2.20 eV) with respect to those of the bulk CN (2.01 eV) and CQDs (2.14 eV). According to the band gap values obtained from the Tauc plots and VB values from the XPS full-VB spectra, the calculated energy gaps between the Fermi level (Ef) and CB are 0.75, 0.57, and 0.45 eV for the bulk CN, CQDs, and CCNTs, respectively. The energy band diagrams of the bulk CN, CQDs, and CCNTs are shown in Fig. S3, S4, and 2c, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2c the Ef of the CCNTs is considerably closer to the CB. The excess electrons from the nitrogen atoms in the CQDs facilitate the upward shift of the Fermi level. The estimated energy band alignments of the CN, CQDs, and CCNTs greatly influence their application in PV devices. Hence, the micro-regional heterostructure that alters the work function of graphite carbon and the CB of CN promotes photocarrier transfer kinetics for improved electron–hole separation [46].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra, which is generated by the electron-hole recombination could be used to determine the transfer and capture rate of photoinduced carriers [47]. The PL spectra of bulk CN, CCNTs, CQDs, and CNNTs are shown in Fig. 2d, and Fig S4. Bulk CN exhibited an emission peak at ∼450 nm which may be due to the recombination of CB electrons with that of VB electrons. In the PL spectra of CCNTs, the peak remained at the same wavelength, however, the intensity of peak decreased suggesting that the embedded CQDs suppressed the charge recombination rate. The PL intensities decreased in order of bulk CN > CNNTs > CQDs > CCNTs. Moreover, in CCNT, the sp [2]-hybridized nitrogen functions as an electron buffer, which facilitates the transfer of excited electrons and π-electrons in the micro-region, thereby preventing electron–hole recombination [48].

Furthermore, XPS analysis was performed to investigate the chemical states and interactions in the CQDs and CN in CCNTs. Fig. 3 and Fig. S5-S6 show the XPS survey spectra of the bulk CN, CCNTs, CNNTs, and CQDs. Accordingly, the CQDs, bulk CN, CCNTs, and CNNTs contain carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The high-resolution C1s spectra of the CQDs, bulk CN, CCNTs, and CNNTs can be resolved into two peaks corresponding to ∼285 and 287 eV; they were assigned to carbon (in the form of C–N and OCO, respectively). The N1s spectra of the nitrogen-rich CQDs indicate the presence of CN–C (N1), (C)3–N (N2), and N–H (N3). Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of the CQDs, bulk CN, and CCNTs; the broad bands at 3000–3300 and 1200–1650 and the peak at 808 cm−1 are assigned to –CN stretching vibrations, aromatic C–N heterocycles, and s-triazine rings, respectively [49].

The current density–voltage (J–V) curves of the CCNTs, CQD, CNNTs, and bulk CN-sensitized devices are compared in Fig. 5 a. The measured PV parameters (i.e. the open-circuit voltage (V
oc ), short-circuit current (J
sc ), fill factor (FF), and PCE) are provided in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, Device-IV has a V
oc of 0.69 V, a J
sc of 0.52 mA/cm−2, an FF of 52%, and a PCE of 0.19% (average value). Device-II and Device-III have better PV parameters and PCEs than Device-IV. More specifically, they have PCEs of 0.59% and 0.49%, respectively. Device-I has a PCE of 1.01% with a V
oc of 1.65, J
sc of 0.89, and an FF of 69%; these values are the highest among all reported carbon-based QDSCs [29, 30, 32, 50, 51–55]. The increase in V
oc from 0.69 to 1.65 V is responsible for the remarkable improvement in the PCE of Device-I compared to the values of all other devices. g-C3N4 NTs acted as block layers to suppress the recombination and promote the separation of electrons and holes and build a type-II band alignment between TiO2 and CCNTs. The higher Voc primarily depends on favorable band alignment with a negligible barrier between the CB of TiO2 and the CCNTs, which is critical and allows for efficient injection of electrons to the wide band-gap semiconductor without sacrificing the Voc. The improved Voc can be attributed to the formation of type-II band alignment between CCNTs and TiO2, which accelerated the separation of photogenerated carriers and resulted in higher Voc [56,57].

The CV was performed to determine the HOMO–LUMO energy levels of all materials (Fig. S7). The calculated LUMO and HOMO levels of the CCNTs are −3.99 and 6.64 eV, respectively (Fig. 6 b). CCNTs photoactive layer functions as an electron blocking layer, which promotes the transfer of excited electrons as indicated by the shift in the CB of CCNTs close to the CB of TiO2 (to absorb photons and release electrons from the LUMO (−3.99 eV) to the CB of TiO2 (−4.21 eV)). The holes move from the HOMO level of the CCNTs (−6.64 eV) toward the redox potential (−4.78 eV) of I−/
I 3 −
couples, thereby resulting in improved device characteristics compared to those of the other devices. These results are consistent with the external quantum efficiency (EQE) results; the EQE of Device-I is significantly improved in the 320–730 nm range (Fig. 5b). The EQE spectrum looks similar to the absorption spectra of light-harvesting materials (Fig. 1a and Fig. S1). As show in Fig. 5b, Device-I has a higher EQE than all the other devices at longer wavelengths. Hence, many long-wavelength photons are absorbed. The enhanced EQE at higher wavelengths indicates the better current collection efficiency of the device [58].

The quick light responses and stability characteristics of all the prepared devices are compared in Fig. 5c. Upon light illumination (100 mW cm−2), the current rapidly increases; it rapidly decreases after the light is cut off. This significant increase in the photocurrent demonstrates the effective light-harvesting properties of the CCNTs-sensitized device. The effective charge separation under visible light limits the electron–hole recombination, which improves the device performance [59]. The better performance of Device-I with respect to the other devices can be ascribed to the CQD-embedded tubular structural design of the CCNTs. The interfacial interactions among CQDs and CN adjust the electronic structure of the CCNTs, which results in a smaller photocarrier transfer barrier [60].

Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were recorded to understand the charge transport properties and interfaces of the devices. Fig. 7 shows the Nyquist plots of all the prepared devices at different forward bias voltages with fitted curves; the used circuit was based on QDSCs (Fig. 7a Inset: electrical equivalent circuit) [61]. The corresponding EIS parameters including the chemical capacitance (Cμ) and recombination resistance (Rrec) were extracted; Cμ represents the change in the electron density, which depends on the Ef of TiO2; Rrec represents charge recombination at the photoanode/electrolyte interface [62].

As illustrated in Fig. 8 a, the similar values at different bias voltages of all the devices reveal that the light-absorbing materials have negligible influences on the CB edges of TiO2 electron acceptors. Moreover, a significant difference in the Rrec values at the same bias voltage can be observed. As shown in Fig. 8b, the Rrec values have the following order: Device-I > Device-II > Device-II > Device-IV. Moreover, the Rrec values are inversely proportional to the charge recombination rate (CRR) at the photoanode/electrolyte interface. In general, the CRR at photoanode/electrolyte interfaces is determined by the recombination of photogenerated electrons in TiO2 with electrolyte and the back flow of electrons at the TiO2/electrolyte interfaces [61]. Thus, the higher Rrec value of Device-I relative to those of the other devices can be attributed to the slower back flow of electrons from TiO2 to CCNTs and the faster electron transfer from CCNTs to TiO2, which improves the cell performance. In summary, the use of CCNTs as “green” light harvesters help to suppress charge recombination, which improves the charge collection efficiency and PV performance of a device.

Solar cell stability is crucial for real applications [63]. The operational photostability of all the devices was measured. All the devices were illuminated under 100 mW/cm-2 light for 8 h, and the PV parameters, such as Voc, Jsc, FF, and PCE, were recorded every hour. The recorded results are presented in Fig. 9 . All the devices were stored in the air without sealing. As seen in Fig., the PCEs of Device-II, Device-III, and Device-IV declined continuously, whereas the PCE values of Device-I decrease in the first 2 h from 1.01% to 0.92%, and constant PCE values were maintained thereafter. The superior stability of Device-I could be credited to the midgap states produced by CCNTs which can enhance the charge transfer at the electrolyte/QD interface and reduced recombination with holes or the electrolyte. CCNTs trapped electrons suppressing the charge recombination rate. The separated electrons can be collected via an external circuit. Hence the reduced recombination can improve the photostability of QDSCs [64,65].

In this study, “green” QDSCs based on CCNTs (CQDs-embedded CN NTs) were prepared to build light-harvesting materials with an average PCE of 1.01%; this is the highest value among all carbon-based QDSCs. The exceptional performance of the solar cell device can be credited to the tubular micro-regional heterostructure of the CCNTs. The well-designed architecture of the CCNTs facilitates charge separation. Moreover, the integration of nitrogen-rich CQDs in CCNTs adjusts the band alignment and maximizes the visible light harvest by reducing the energy barrier, which synergistically improves the charge collection efficiency of the device. In addition, the CCNTs-sensitized device showed superior photostability compared to CQDs-, CNNTs-, and bulk CN-sensitized devices. Overall, the sensible combination of CQDs with CN can be used in the form of efficient light harvesters to improve the PV performance of solar cells.
Shahina Riaz: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Soo-Jin Park: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Technology Innovation Program (or Industrial Strategic Technology Development Program) (20012373, Innovative technology for CO2 free hydrogen production using molten catalysts) funded By the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE, Korea). The research was also supported the Korea Industrial Complex Corporation through the Project to Program of Industrial Complex Clusters (IRIC2101, Industry-University-Research joint R&D support) funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE, Korea).
